text: The robotic surgical procedure focuses on maintaining a preperitoneal approach during the dissection of a left direct inguinal hernia. The surgeon performs medial dissection down to the pubic tubercle, colon, and Cooper's ligament, followed by lateral dissection to prevent tension and potential tearing of the peritoneum.
The surgeon identifies the large direct hernia sac and begins to reduce it, ensuring careful separation to avoid pulling the bladder into the sac, especially when using cautery.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection in the inguinal region, noting the absence of an indirect sac which facilitates easier dissection. Inferior dissection is carried out below the obturator space, revealing a typical fat plug. Final lateral dissection is performed to create adequate space for the placement of a 12x16 ProGrip mesh, ensuring good inferior coverage.

The surgeon identifies a lipoma hidden beneath wispy transverse fibers and resects it, ensuring the testicle is not reduced. The final dissection reveals the attenuated transversalis fascia, which is invoked to Cooper's ligament for mesh placement. Further lateral dissection is performed to accommodate the mesh, aiming to prevent seroma formation and provide a scaffold for the mesh in the large direct defect.

The surgeon centers the mesh more laterally than usual for a large indirect defect. Using a remnant suture from the imbrication of the direct sac, the mesh is fixated medially to prevent early migration in the area of weakness. The peritoneal flap is closed and secured by running it back multiple times. An angiocath is used to evacuate gas from the peritoneal flap, ensuring the mesh sits flat.
